target.name: majusanic acid D
target.motivation: Majusanic acid D (1) and majusanin B (2) belong to a novel class of abietane-type diterpenoids that have been isolated in recent years from the roots of Illicium majus. Structurally, they share a densely functionalized trans-decalin framework, including three contiguous stereocenters, two of which are quaternary centers. Angustanoic acid E (6) demonstrates significant cytotoxicities against five human tumor cells. Stimulated by their impressive structures and bioactivities, the authors initiated a synthesis study of majusanic acid D (1) and its derivatives (2–6).

success_criteria.free_text: Total synthesis of majusanic acid D (1) and its derivatives (2-6) in significant yield.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy involves synthesizing callitrisic acid (7) from abietic acid (14) using a modified literature procedure. Then, selective oxidation of activated 3° C-H bonds and activated 2° C-H bonds is performed via an Fe-bTAML complex (12) in the presence of m-CPBA. The overall strategy aims for a unified approach to all known abietane diterpenoids (1–6) inspired by their biogenesis.

thought.background: The authors aimed to synthesize the core structure of abietane diterpenoids, such as callitrisic acid (7). Based on previous reports, they started from commercially available (+)-abietic acid (14).
thought.gap: The initial step involves converting abietic acid to dehydroabietic acid.
thought.decision: To convert abietic acid (14) to dehydroabietic acid (14a), the authors subjected it to high temperature (200 °C) for 4 hours.

outcome.observation: Dehydroabietic acid (14a) was formed. Yield not specified for this step, but overall yield for 10 steps to 7 is 32%.

evidence.text_span: Initially, we wanted to synthesize the core structure of abietane diterpenoids, such as callitrisic acid22 (7). On the basis of previous reports by two independent groups, Pelletier et al. and Alvarez-Manzaneda et al.,24 we have started our synthesis from commercially available $( + ) { } .$ -abietic acid (14). By subjecting abietic acid to a temperature of $2 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for a period of 4 hours resulting in the formation of dehydroabietic acid (14a) (see ESI† ).

thought.background: Dehydroabietic acid (14a) was obtained from abietic acid (14).
thought.gap: The next step is to methylate the carboxylic acid.
thought.decision: To methylate dehydroabietic acid, the authors used dimethyl sulfate.

outcome.observation: Methylation occurred, forming compound (15). Yield not specified for this step.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, the resultant product underwent methylation utilizing dimethyl sulfate $[ ( \mathbf { MeO } ) _ { 2 } \mathbf { SO } _ { 2 } ] _ { \cdot }$ , resulting in the formation of (15).

thought.gap: The ester group needs to be reduced to a primary alcohol.
thought.decision: To reduce the ester (15) to a primary alcohol (16), the authors used LiAlH4.

outcome.observation: The primary alcohol (16) was formed in 96% yield.

evidence.text_span: The subsequent compound (15) was subjected to reduction using $\mathrm { L iAlH _ { 4 } }$ , leading to the formation of the primary alcohol (16) with a yield of $9 6\%$ .

thought.gap: The primary alcohol needs to be oxidized to an aldehyde.
thought.decision: To oxidize the primary alcohol (16) to an aldehyde (17), the authors used PCC.

outcome.observation: Aldehyde (17) was formed in 84% yield (from image).

evidence.text_span: Treatment of (16) with PCC afforded the aldehyde (17)

thought.gap: The next step involves a Baeyer-Villiger oxidation and subsequent saponification to introduce a hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To achieve this, the authors treated (17) with m-chloroperbenzoic acid, followed by saponification with refluxing 2,6-lutidine.

outcome.observation: Compound (18) was obtained, whose physical and spectroscopic properties were identical to those reported in the literature. Yield of 89% from image.

evidence.text_span: which, when treated with mchloroperbenzoic acid, gave the formate (see ESI† for details), which aer saponication with reuxing 2,6-lutidine yielded (18), whose physical and spectroscopic properties were identical to those reported in the literature.20b

thought.gap: A C-4 hydroxyl group needs to be installed regio- and diastereoselectively.
thought.decision: To install the C-4 hydroxyl group, the authors treated (18) with BH3·SMe2 and oxidized the resultant alkyl borane with H2O2.

outcome.observation: Compound (19) was generated regio- and diastereoselectively in 72% yield with a dr > 10:1.

evidence.text_span: The C-4 hydroxyl group in (19) was installed by treatment of (18) with ${  f BH } _ { 3 } \cdot {  f SMe } _ { 2 }$ and oxidation of the resultant alkyl borane with $\mathbf { H } _ { 2 } \mathbf { O } _ { 2 }$ to generate (19) regio- and diastereoselectively in $7 2\%$ yield and its primary alcohol was converted to the corresponding aldehyde (20) by PCC oxidation in $8 2\%$ yield.

thought.gap: The primary alcohol needs to be converted to the corresponding aldehyde.
thought.decision: To convert the primary alcohol (19) to aldehyde (20), the authors used PCC oxidation.

outcome.observation: Aldehyde (20) was obtained in 82% yield.

evidence.text_span: and its primary alcohol was converted to the corresponding aldehyde (20) by PCC oxidation in $8 2\%$ yield.

thought.gap: A C-4 quaternary chiral center needs to be installed, followed by oxidation to a carboxylic acid.
thought.decision: To install the C-4 quaternary chiral center, the authors treated aldehyde (20) with tBuOK in THF, and the resultant enolate was reacted with MeI. This was followed by a Pinnick oxidation to introduce a carboxylic acid group, leading to callitrisic acid (7).

outcome.observation: Callitrisic acid (7) was obtained in 90% yield (from image).

evidence.text_span: The C-4 quaternary chiral center was installed by treating aldehyde (20) with $\mathbf { \ t _ { B u O K } }$ in THF, and the resultant enolate was reacted with MeI followed by a Pinnick oxidation introduced a carboxylic acid group in callitrisic acid (7).

thought.gap: The carboxylic acid needs to be methylated to form methyl callitrisate.
thought.decision: To methylate callitrisic acid (7), the authors used dimethyl sulfate.

outcome.observation: Methyl callitrisate (8) was formed in 98% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, callitrisic acid (7) was methylated using dimethyl sulfate $[ ( \mathbf { MeO } ) _ { 2 } \mathbf { SO } _ { 2 } ]$ , resulting in the formation of methyl callitrisate (8) in $9 8\%$ yield (Scheme 1).

thought.background: Methyl callitrisate (8) was obtained. The goal is to perform selective C-H bond oxidation.
thought.gap: Optimizing the reaction conditions for the Fe(III)-bTAML catalyzed C-H oxidation.
thought.decision: The authors performed a catalytic reaction by introducing m-CPBA (5 equiv) via a syringe pump into a solution containing (12) (1 mol%) and substrate (8) (0.2 mmol scale) in an 80% CH3CN-20% K2HPO4 (aq) solvent system for 15 minutes.

outcome.observation: This condition yielded the benzylic ketone intermediate (22) with a 70% yield, but the target product (13) was not formed (0%). The starting material (8) was present in 30%.

evidence.text_span: We then performed catalytic reaction by introducing m-CPBA (5 equiv) via a syringe pump at a rate of $1 0 0 ^ { - } 2 0 0 ~ \mu \mathrm { L } ~ \mathrm { h } ^ { - 1 }$ into a solution containing (12) $( 1 \mathrm { m o l\% } )$ and substrate (8) (0.2 mmol scale) in an $8 0 \%$ $\mathrm { C H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { C N } { \mathrm { - } }$ $2 0\%$ ${ \mathrm { K } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { H P O } } _ { 4 }$ (aq) solvent system (Table 1).5 Following this protocol for 15 minutes yielded the benzylic ketone intermediate (22) with a $7 0\%$ yield (entry 1, Table 1).

thought.background: The previous attempt for 15 minutes yielded the benzylic ketone intermediate (22) with 70% yield, but not the desired product (13).
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of longer reaction time on the product distribution.
thought.decision: The reaction time was extended to 30 minutes under the same conditions.

outcome.observation: After 30 minutes, the desired product (13) was obtained in 20% yield, but the benzylic ketone intermediate (22) was still present in 57%, and starting material (8) in 23% (entry 2, Table 1).

evidence.text_span: Extending the reaction time to 1 hour with ketone (22) produced the overoxidized product (13) in an $8 5\%$ yield (see $\mathbf { ESI\dagger }$ for details). Similarly, callitrisic acid methyl ester (8) was converted directly to the over-oxidized product (13) in $8 2\%$ yield under the same conditions for $^ { 1 . 5 \mathrm { ~ h ~ } }$ (entry 5, Table 1).

thought.background: The previous attempts in CH3CN/H2O showed incomplete conversion and side product formation.
thought.gap: Explore different solvent systems to improve selectivity and yield.
thought.decision: The authors tested THF/H2O (4:1) as the solvent system for 30 minutes.

outcome.observation: In THF/H2O, the desired product (13) was obtained in 46% yield, with 25% of intermediate (22) and 29% of starting material (8) (entry 3, Table 1).

evidence.text_span:
Table 1 Optimization of Fe(III)-bTAML Catalysed $\mathsf { Csp } ^ { 3 }  oldsymbol { - } \mathsf { H }$ oxidation
Entry 3 THF/H2O (4:1) 30 46:25:29

thought.background: Previous solvent systems (CH3CN/H2O, THF/H2O) did not give optimal results.
thought.gap: Further explore solvent effects on the oxidation reaction.
thought.decision: The authors tested DMF/H2O (4:1) as the solvent system for 30 minutes.

outcome.observation: In DMF/H2O, the desired product (13) was obtained in 32% yield, with 20% of intermediate (22) and 48% of starting material (8) (entry 4, Table 1).

evidence.text_span:
Table 1 Optimization of Fe(III)-bTAML Catalysed $\mathsf { Csp } ^ { 3 }  oldsymbol { - } \mathsf { H }$ oxidation
Entry 4 DMF/H2O (4:1) 30 32:20:48

thought.background: Previous attempts showed that longer reaction times and specific solvent systems might be beneficial for complete conversion to the over-oxidized product (13).
thought.gap: Identify optimal conditions for direct conversion of methyl callitrisate (8) to the over-oxidized product (13).
thought.decision: The authors used CH3CN/H2O as solvent, extended the reaction time to 90 minutes, and maintained other conditions (1 mol% catalyst, 5 equiv m-CPBA).

outcome.observation: Methyl callitrisate (8) was converted directly to the over-oxidized product (13) in 82% yield. No intermediate (22) or starting material (8) was detected (entry 5, Table 1).

evidence.text_span: Similarly, callitrisic acid methyl ester (8) was converted directly to the over-oxidized product (13) in $8 2\%$ yield under the same conditions for $^ { 1 . 5 \mathrm { ~ h ~ } }$ (entry 5, Table 1).

thought.background: The optimal conditions for converting (8) to (13) were found (entry 5, Table 1). Now, the authors want to explore the effect of solvent ratio.
thought.gap: Investigate if a different CH3CN/H2O ratio affects the yield.
thought.decision: The authors changed the solvent ratio to CH3CN/H2O (2:1) and ran the reaction for 90 minutes.

outcome.observation: The yield of (13) decreased to 75%, with 15% of intermediate (22) still present (entry 6, Table 1).

evidence.text_span:
Table 1 Optimization of Fe(III)-bTAML Catalysed $\mathsf { Csp } ^ { 3 }  oldsymbol { - } \mathsf { H }$ oxidation
Entry 6 CH3CN/H2O (2:1) 90 75:15:0

thought.background: The optimal conditions for converting (8) to (13) were found (entry 5, Table 1). Now, the authors want to explore the effect of solvent ratio with DMF.
thought.gap: Investigate if a different DMF/H2O ratio affects the yield.
thought.decision: The authors changed the solvent ratio to DMF/H2O (2:1) and ran the reaction for 90 minutes.

outcome.observation: The yield of (13) was 61%, with no intermediate (22) or starting material (8) detected (entry 7, Table 1).

evidence.text_span:
Table 1 Optimization of Fe(III)-bTAML Catalysed $\mathsf { Csp } ^ { 3 }  oldsymbol { - } \mathsf { H }$ oxidation
Entry 7 DMF/H2O (2:1) 90 61:0:0

thought.background: The optimal conditions for converting (8) to (13) were found (entry 5, Table 1). Now, the authors want to explore the effect of using only water as solvent.
thought.gap: Investigate if water alone can serve as a suitable solvent for the oxidation.
thought.decision: The authors used H2O as the solvent and ran the reaction for 90 minutes.

outcome.observation: No desired product (13) was formed. 23% of intermediate (22) and 77% of starting material (8) remained (entry 8, Table 1).

evidence.text_span:
Table 1 Optimization of Fe(III)-bTAML Catalysed $\mathsf { Csp } ^ { 3 }  oldsymbol { - } \mathsf { H }$ oxidation
Entry 8 H2O 90 0:23:77

thought.background: The optimal conditions for converting (8) to (13) were found (entry 5, Table 1). Now, the authors want to explore the effect of using only acetonitrile as solvent.
thought.gap: Investigate if acetonitrile alone can serve as a suitable solvent for the oxidation.
thought.decision: The authors used CH3CN as the solvent and ran the reaction for 90 minutes.

outcome.observation: No desired product (13) was formed. 31% of intermediate (22) and 69% of starting material (8) remained (entry 9, Table 1).

evidence.text_span:
Table 1 Optimization of Fe(III)-bTAML Catalysed $\mathsf { Csp } ^ { 3 }  oldsymbol { - } \mathsf { H }$ oxidation
Entry 9 CH3CN 90 0:31:69

thought.background: The over-oxidized product (13) was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: The ketone group in (13) needs to be reduced to a tertiary alcohol, and the methyl ester needs to be hydrolyzed to a carboxylic acid to form majusanic acid D (1).
thought.decision: To achieve this, the authors performed a Wolff–Kishner reduction with hydrazine hydrate to reduce the ketone, followed by hydrolysis of the methyl ester using LiOH and KOH in hot methanol.

outcome.observation: Majusanic acid D (1) was obtained in 88% yield (from image).

evidence.text_span: Our next synthetic target was majusanin B (2) and its derivatives (3–6, Fig. 1). With (13) in hand, we next explored Wolff– Kishner reduction25 with hydrazine hydrate $\left( \mathbf { N } _ { 2 } \mathbf { H } _ { 4 } \cdot \mathbf { H } _ { 2 } \mathbf { O } ight)$ provided the tertiary alcohol in (23) in $7 7\%$ yield, which underwent elimination by reaction with MsCl in the presence of $\mathrm { Et } _ { 3 } \mathrm { N }$ in $\mathrm { CH } _ { 2 } \mathrm { Cl } _ { 2 }$ to give (24) in $8 8\%$ yield. ... Lastly, hydrolysis of methyl ester of (13), (23), (24), and (25) using LiOH and KOH in hot methanol27 completed the total synthesis of majusanic acid D (1), angustanoic acid F (4), angustanoic acid E (6) and angustanoic acid G (3) respectively (Scheme 4).

thought.background: The over-oxidized product (13) was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: The ketone group in (13) needs to be reduced to a tertiary alcohol to form (23).
thought.decision: To achieve this, the authors performed a Wolff–Kishner reduction with hydrazine hydrate.

outcome.observation: The tertiary alcohol (23) was obtained in 77% yield.

evidence.text_span: Our next synthetic target was majusanin B (2) and its derivatives (3–6, Fig. 1). With (13) in hand, we next explored Wolff– Kishner reduction25 with hydrazine hydrate $\left( \mathbf { N } _ { 2 } \mathbf { H } _ { 4 } \cdot \mathbf { H } _ { 2 } \mathbf { O } ight)$ provided the tertiary alcohol in (23) in $7 7\%$ yield

thought.gap: The tertiary alcohol needs to undergo elimination to form an olefin (24).
thought.decision: To achieve elimination, the authors reacted (23) with MsCl in the presence of Et3N in CH2Cl2.

outcome.observation: Compound (24) was obtained in 88% yield.

evidence.text_span: which underwent elimination by reaction with MsCl in the presence of $\mathrm { Et } _ { 3 } \mathrm { N }$ in $\mathrm { CH } _ { 2 } \mathrm { Cl } _ { 2 }$ to give (24) in $8 8\%$ yield.

thought.gap: The olefin group in (24) needs to be oxidatively cleaved to furnish an aromatic acetophenone (25).
thought.decision: To achieve oxidative cleavage, the authors used ozonolysis.

outcome.observation: Aromatic acetophenone (25) was furnished in 76% moderate yield.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent oxidative cleavage of (24) with ozonolysis to furnish aromatic acetophenone (25) in $7 6\%$ moderate yield.

thought.gap: The olefin group in (24) needs to be dihydroxylated to form a diol (26).
thought.decision: To achieve dihydroxylation, the authors used OsO4/NMO.

outcome.observation: Diol (26) was obtained in 92% yield.

evidence.text_span: Dihydroxylation of the olen group of the styrene derivative in (24) with $\mathrm { O s O _ { 4 } / N M O ^ { 26 } }$ gave diol (26) in $9 2\%$ yield (Scheme 2).

thought.gap: The methyl ester in (23) needs to be reduced to a primary alcohol to form angustanol (5).
thought.decision: To achieve this, the authors used LiAlH4 reduction.

outcome.observation: Angustanol (5) was obtained in 89% yield (from image).

evidence.text_span: Later, we moved ahead for the collective total synthesis of abietane diterpenoids 1–6 shown in Fig. 1. Reduction of (23) and (26) via $\mathrm { L iAlH _ { 4 } }$ -reduction ensures the total synthesis of angustanol (5) and majusanin B (2) (Scheme 3).

thought.gap: The methyl ester in (26) needs to be reduced to a primary alcohol to form majusanin B (2).
thought.decision: To achieve this, the authors used LiAlH4 reduction.

outcome.observation: Majusanin B (2) was obtained in 92% yield (from image).

evidence.text_span: Later, we moved ahead for the collective total synthesis of abietane diterpenoids 1–6 shown in Fig. 1. Reduction of (23) and (26) via $\mathrm { L iAlH _ { 4 } }$ -reduction ensures the total synthesis of angustanol (5) and majusanin B (2) (Scheme 3).

thought.gap: The methyl ester in (23) needs to be hydrolyzed to a carboxylic acid to form angustanoic acid F (4).
thought.decision: To achieve this, the authors used LiOH and KOH in hot methanol.

outcome.observation: Angustanoic acid F (4) was obtained in 76% yield (from image).

evidence.text_span: Lastly, hydrolysis of methyl ester of (13), (23), (24), and (25) using LiOH and KOH in hot methanol27 completed the total synthesis of majusanic acid D (1), angustanoic acid F (4), angustanoic acid E (6) and angustanoic acid G (3) respectively (Scheme 4).

thought.gap: The methyl ester in (24) needs to be hydrolyzed to a carboxylic acid to form angustanoic acid E (6).
thought.decision: To achieve this, the authors used LiOH and KOH in hot methanol.

outcome.observation: Angustanoic acid E (6) was obtained in 82% yield (from image).

evidence.text_span: Lastly, hydrolysis of methyl ester of (13), (23), (24), and (25) using LiOH and KOH in hot methanol27 completed the total synthesis of majusanic acid D (1), angustanoic acid F (4), angustanoic acid E (6) and angustanoic acid G (3) respectively (Scheme 4).

thought.gap: The methyl ester in (25) needs to be hydrolyzed to a carboxylic acid to form angustanoic acid G (3).
thought.decision: To achieve this, the authors used LiOH and KOH in hot methanol.

outcome.observation: Angustanoic acid G (3) was obtained in 87% yield (from image).

evidence.text_span: Lastly, hydrolysis of methyl ester of (13), (23), (24), and (25) using LiOH and KOH in hot methanol27 completed the total synthesis of majusanic acid D (1), angustanoic acid F (4), angustanoic acid E (6) and angustanoic acid G (3) respectively (Scheme 4).

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the synthesis of (+)-callitrisic acid (7) in 10 steps from abietic acid (14).

validation.significance: The authors completed the total syntheses of all known abietane diterpenoids (1–6) through a unified strategy. Key features include the synthesis of (+)-callitrisic acid (7) in 10 steps with 32% overall yield and the demonstration of the synthetic utility of Fe-bTAML complex catalyzed biomimetic C–H bond oxidation in the total synthesis of complex natural products.

source.title: Total synthesis of naturally occurring abietane diterpenoids via a late-stage Fe(iii)-bTAML catalysed Csp3-H functionalization.
source.authors: Munda M, Chatterjee D, Majhi M, Biswas S, Pal D, Bisai A.
